description:
You are a monitoring-operations specialist who has just received a feed of raw events
    in the comma-separated file 

        /home/user/data/event_feed.csv

    The Network Command Centre (NCC) only wants to see the most urgent situations,
    formatted in a very strict three-column layout that can be ingested by their dashboard.

    Your job inside the terminal is to:

    1. Create the directory
           /home/user/alerts
       if it does not already exist.

    2. From the file /home/user/data/event_feed.csv select ONLY the rows whose
       fourth column (the “severity” column) is the exact string

           CRITICAL

       (all-caps, no leading/trailing spaces).

    3. From those CRITICAL rows extract:
         • column-2  → the ISO-8601 timestamp
         • column-4  → the severity (which will always be the literal string CRITICAL)

       You must perform the extraction with standard UNIX column tools
       (e.g. cut, paste, awk, etc.; the test harness does not care which, but it will
       check that the final file is correct).

    4. Assemble a new three-column text file named

           /home/user/alerts/alert_stream.log

       whose contents are exactly:

           <timestamp>|<severity>|ALERT

       …one alert per line, **in the same order that the original CRITICAL rows
       appear in event_feed.csv**.  There must be NO header line.  Each line **must**
       terminate with a single UNIX newline character (`\n`).  The third column must be
       the literal word “ALERT” (all caps).

       Example of a single correct line:
           2024-01-14T09:18:05Z|CRITICAL|ALERT

    5. Leave the source file (/home/user/data/event_feed.csv) unchanged.

    The automated checker will verify:

       • the directory /home/user/alerts exists;
       • the file /home/user/alerts/alert_stream.log exists
         and is readable by the current (non-root) user;
       • alert_stream.log contains **exactly** the three CRITICAL events in the order
         they appeared in the CSV, formatted as 
             timestamp|CRITICAL|ALERT
         with no extra spaces, columns, blank lines or headers.

    You do NOT need to print the file to the console—just make sure it is created with
    the correct contents on disk.  Good luck!
truth:
<!--
        Ground-truth verification data for the automated tests.
        This data must NOT be revealed to the agent.
    -->

    Initial state:
      1. The file /home/user/data/event_feed.csv is already present with exactly
         the following eight lines (including the header and final newline):

event_id,timestamp,service,severity,message
1001,2024-01-14T09:15:22Z,auth,INFO,User login successful
1002,2024-01-14T09:18:05Z,database,CRITICAL,Database connection lost
1003,2024-01-14T09:20:47Z,web,WARN,High latency detected
1004,2024-01-14T09:22:10Z,filesystem,CRITICAL,Disk space critically low
1005,2024-01-14T09:25:33Z,auth,INFO,User logout
1006,2024-01-14T09:30:01Z,network,MAJOR,Packet loss above threshold
1007,2024-01-14T09:35:17Z,security,CRITICAL,Multiple failed logins
1008,2024-01-14T09:40:00Z,backup,INFO,Backup completed

    Desired final state AFTER the agent completes the task:
      1. Directory /home/user/alerts exists (755 or looser perms OK).
      2. File /home/user/alerts/alert_stream.log exists (644 or looser perms OK)
         and its exact byte-for-byte content is:

2024-01-14T09:18:05Z|CRITICAL|ALERT
2024-01-14T09:22:10Z|CRITICAL|ALERT
2024-01-14T09:35:17Z|CRITICAL|ALERT

         The file ends with a final newline (0x0A).

    The checker will:
      • stat(2) the directory and file;
      • read the file and compare it to the reference content above;
      • ensure no extra or missing lines exist.
name: task_000000_bb50bb64
